questions.questionText: Under the McNaughten rule, a person is legally insane and NOT criminally responsible if:
questions.optionB: At the time of the act, they were suffering from such a defect of reason from disease of the mind that they did not know the nature/quality of the act or did not know it was wrong
questions.explanation: McNaughten rule (1843): legal test of insanity. Person is not criminally responsible if, at the time of the act, due to disease of mind: (1) Did not know the NATURE and QUALITY of the act (cognitive limb), or (2) Did not know what they were doing was WRONG. Section 84 IPC (now BNS Sec 22): nothing is an offence done by a person of unsound mind. Burden of proof: defense must prove insanity. Irresistible impulse: NOT accepted as defense in India. Durham rule: broader — product of mental disease. Reference: Reddy — Criminal Responsibility.

questions.questionText: Testamentary capacity refers to the mental capacity to:
questions.optionB: Make a valid will (person must understand: nature of making a will, extent of property, claims of people who should benefit)
questions.explanation: Testamentary capacity: mental ability to make a valid will (testament). Requirements: person must understand — (1) WHAT they are doing (making a will), (2) EXTENT of their property, (3) WHO the natural beneficiaries are (moral claims). Can make a will during lucid intervals in mental illness. Unsound mind at time of making will → will is invalid. Civil capacity vs criminal responsibility: different standards. Contractual capacity: mental ability to enter into agreements. Fitness to stand trial: can they understand proceedings and instruct counsel? Reference: Reddy — Testamentary Capacity.

questions.optionB: Factitious disorder — intentional production or feigning of physical/psychological symptoms to assume the sick role (hospital hopper/peregrinating patient)
questions.explanation: Munchausen syndrome: factitious disorder — deliberate production of symptoms/signs to assume sick role. Motivation: internal psychological need (NOT external gain — differentiates from malingering). Features: hospital hopping, multiple surgeries, dramatic presentations, extensive medical knowledge, pathological lying (pseudologia fantastica). Munchausen by proxy: caregiver (usually mother) fabricates/induces illness in child — form of child abuse. Malingering: conscious simulation of disease for external gain (avoiding work, insurance, legal). Not a psychiatric disorder. Reference: Reddy — Munchausen.
